title: 'The Morning Show' will bring Apple TV's first prestige drama to an end in 2027
content:
Jennifer Aniston and Reese Witherspoon helped bolster fledging Apple TV with "The Morning Show" in 2019. The final season of the award-winning drama has been announced, bringing an era to a close.


Reese Wotherspoon (left) and Jennifer Aniston on stage at the launch of Apple TV+ in 2019 - image credit: Apple. 
Eddy Cue wanted "The Morning Show." Long before the service was announced in March 2019, or debuted in November that year, Cue was looking for shows that would bring the streamer attention, and "The Morning Show" was being shopped around.
Created by Jay Carson and developed by Kerry Ehrin, the drama was being executive produced by its stars, Jennifer Aniston and Reese Witherspoon. "I thought the show was amazing," Cue later told Variety, "but... I came down to the realization that we weren't going to get it, because somebody would always offer more."

 Continue Reading on AppleInsider | Discuss on our Forums
creator: William Gallagher <wg@williamgallagher.com>

title: OpenAI relaunches Apple Health-connected ChatGPT feature with expanded access
content: OpenAI is relaunching Health in ChatGPT, the AI-powered feature that connects to Apple Health on iPhone. The relaunch comes with expanded access and a big step forward in results.
creator: Zac Hall

title: OpenAI sued after ChatGPT used religious faith to convince a man to not talk to a doctor
content:
ChatGPT leveraged faith to convince a man with a cardiac issue stay home instead of seeking medical help. Now, facing years of recovery and profound financial damage, the man is suing OpenAI over the ordeal.


ChatGPT isn't a medical professional. Credit: OpenAI
In a lawsuit filed in San Francisco Superior Court, Florida pastor Scott Winters argues that ChatGPT used his faith against him. After asking the chatbot about his symptoms, he was told that they were likely "another minor piece of the long story" and that "God did not design your body to endlessly fail."
After going back and forth with ChatGPT's Health feature for six weeks, Winters finally spoke to a real medical professional. He was then diagnosed with a dangerous blockage of arteries in both of his lungs.

 Continue Reading on AppleInsider | Discuss on our Forums
creator: Oliver Haslam <oliver@monkeymanmedia.com>

title: New Geekbench 7 benchmark adds AI workloads, better reflects 2026 computer use cases
content:
Primate Labs released Geekbench 7 for macOS, iOS, Android, Windows, and Linux, redesigning its cross-platform benchmark to better reflect how current software uses CPUs and GPUs.

Geekbench 7
The update changes Geekbench's multi-core methodology and adds workloads for artificial intelligence, media processing, gaming, and content creation. Primate Labs says the revisions account for increasingly demanding computing tasks and the larger data sets handled by modern devices.
Geekbench 7 remains free for personal use.

 Continue Reading on AppleInsider | Discuss on our Forums
creator: Andrew Orr <andreworr@appleinsider.com>

title: Geekbench 7 Launches With Redesigned Multi-Core and GPU Benchmarks
content:
Primate Labs today announced the launch of Geekbench 7, the newest version of the popular benchmarking suite for CPU and GPU performance.


There's a redesigned multi-core benchmark in Geekbench 7 that's meant to better represent how applications work in the real world. A workload only runs in multi-threaded mode if the task it models runs multi-threaded in real applications.

Primate Labs says users can expect a multi-core score that's a more relevant measure of how a device performs when doing actual work.

Along with updates to multi-core testing, a revamped GPU benchmark focuses on machine learning and content creation to reflect how GPUs are used today. The GPU benchmark has tests that track faces and apply real-time filter effects to video, upscale images with machine learning, and blur backgrounds in video conferencing streams. New GPU image editing and synthesis tests are available, like RAW image processing, LUT-based video color grading, path tracing, and fluid simulation.

Nvidia's CUDA is once again a supported API alongside OpenCL, Vulkan, and Metal.

The new version of Geekbench uses more challenging data sets to better reflect how people use their computers today. The File Compression and PDF Viewer workloads have more variation, and developer and image processing tests have more assets and different image formats.

Geekbench 7 adds media workloads for testing audio and video encoding, decoding, and processing. The Whisper speech recognition, AV1, and Opus tests mimic video conferencing, screen sharing, and content consumption workloads.

There's also a Game Physics workload that uses the Jolt Physics engine used by popular video games. Primate Labs added an expanded Photo Editor test with a richer set of real-world edits, and the Photo Library workload supports importing and processing image formats like JPEG XL and DNG.

is available for iOS, macOS, and other platforms. The app remains free for personal use, and the Pro version is 20 percent off the regular $99 price through August 6.
Tags: Geekbench, Primate Labs

The MacBook Pro is always an excellent pick for anyone looking to get a professional laptop they’ll use for years to come. But when it comes with these specs, we can’t help but recommend it even more loudly, even when the price is $3,599. First of all, it comes with the M5 Pro 18-Core chip paired with a 20-Core GPU that packs a Neural Accelerator in every core. Then, it is backed up by a jaw-dropping 64GB of RAM and a super-speedy 1TB SSD. Whatever you want to do with it, this machine will handle every task you throw at it.
The laptop features a gorgeous 16-inch Liquid Retina XDR display with a smooth 120Hz refresh rate and up to 1600 nits of brightness. Connectivity is not something you’ll ever complain about either, as the laptop comes packed with Thunderbolt 5 for blazing 120 Gb/s transfers, plus Wi-Fi 7, Bluetooth 6, and MagSafe 3. This laptop’s battery life hits 24 hours, so you can leave the house without a charger any day.
Snag this impressive pro machine for $400 off before this deal expires over at B&H. You won’t need to upgrade for a very long time once you buy this MacBook Pro.

title: Apple TV is branching out with a self-help eight-part dating docuseries
content:
Apple TV has announced an upcoming documentary series, featuring New York Times bestselling author and renowned relationship expert Esther Perel, that sets out to help couples find love where they least expect it.


Apple TV is getting a new show
The new series, titled "The Last Person on Earth," will follow couples who, on the face of it, appear to be opposites. They'll then be sent to one of the planet's most romantic destinations to put into practice tips and guidance provided by Perel and a team of experts.
Apple TV has yet to confirm when the new show will debut, nor whether all eight episodes will be made available simultaneously. The streamer tends to release the first two or three episodes of new shows before settling into a cadence of a new episode each week.

title: Opera One Browser Adds Vertical Tabs - Should Safari Follow?
content:
Opera One browser just pushed an update adding a Vertical Tabs option that moves the tab bar from the top of the window to a resizable panel down the left-hand side.


The feature aims to solve the perennial horizontal tab problem where too many open tabs makes each one shrink to an unreadable sliver of the webpage's favicon. 

By contrast, in the vertical layout, page titles always stay legible. Opera's existing Tab Islands feature – where tabs that came from the same page or browsing session are grouped into a collapsible section in the tab strip – carries over from the standard view, and the panel collapses when screen space is tight.

Opera has also built Google Lens directly into the start page search bar with this update, so now an image search can be started by uploading a file, dragging and dropping, pasting from the clipboard, or entering a URL. Both features are live now in Opera One.

Opera is a late arrival to the vertical phenomenon that has slowly been making inroads across the wider browser landscape. Microsoft made vertical tabs generally available in Edge back in May 2021, and the feature has been popular with users on both Windows and macOS.

March 2025 with Firefox 136, which introduced vertical tabs as part of a redesigned sidebar. Firefox also allows the strip to be moved to the right edge of the window, if that's your preferred style.

April, making full page titles readable and tab groups easier to manage, even at double-digit tab counts. 

Tag: Opera Browser

title: US Mint's limited edition Steve Jobs coin is no longer quite so limited
content:
After selling out the entire stock in 11 minutes in March, the US Mint's commemorative Steve Jobs coin was briefly back on sale on Thursday.


Image Credit: United States Mint
It still only vaguely looks like Steve Jobs, but it's intended to be him and to commemorate American innovation. But stop us if you've heard this one before, as the coin was released in May 2026.
The US Mint's listing at the time said that it was a limited pressing, and the release schedule page still says that. It's conceivable that part of the initial pressing was held back for some reason, but that would be the first time that was done in the American innovation line.

title: 5 Reasons to Buy the iPhone 17 Now Instead of Waiting for iPhone 18
content:
Apple's iPhone 18 Pro models and its first foldable are less than two months from being unveiled. That may sound like a reason to sit tight if you're looking to upgrade from an iPhone 16 or older series, but between looming price hikes and Apple's new split-launch calendar, there's a decent case for grabbing an iPhone 17 series model right now. Here are five reasons in favor of putting your order in today.


1. iPhone Price Hikes Almost Certain

Analysts suggest the iPhone 18 Pro and Pro Max could start at $1,299 and $1,399, respectively. That would represent a $200 jump over their predecessors, with rising memory and storage costs (i.e. the AI buildout) largely to blame. Meanwhile, the foldable iPhone price could start at north of $2,000. Apple has already increased the price of all  of its iPhone models in Japan. The iPhone 17, by contrast, is still selling in the U.S. and other regions at the price Apple set last September (from $799). At this point, upcoming price hikes for the next-generation models seem inevitable, and there's no guarantee that the iPhone 17 series will be spared.

2. Some iPhone 18 Models Still a While Away

Under Apple's expected new split-launch strategy, only the iPhone 18 Pro models and the foldable arrive this September. The regular iPhone 18, lower-cost iPhone 18e, and iPhone Air 2 aren't expected until spring 2027, so anyone holding out for the regular iPhone 17's direct successor will be sat waiting for several months longer than usual.

3. The Base iPhone 18 Could Be a "Downgrade"

One leaker has repeatedly claimed that Apple will reduce the standard iPhone 18's display and chip specifications to control costs, which could see it potentially sharing more components with the cheaper iPhone 18e. And even with a rumored bump to 9GB of RAM, the lower-end models reportedly still won't support two new iOS 27 features. Nothing is certain yet, but if Apple is cutting corners for its next more affordable and base models, then the iPhone 17 or iPhone 17e could be the better-specced option, especially given the expected price difference.

4. Some iPhone 18 Upgrades Remain Uncertain

Several of the more eye-catching iPhone 18 Pro rumors may not play out at launch. An early report claiming that the iPhone 18 Pro would ditch the Dynamic Island for a hole-punch camera has completely lost steam, while the foldable iPhone's rumored 24-megapixel under-display camera may not be ready this year, and a truly crease-free folding screen looks more like "nearly crease-free" in practice. In other words, waiting doesn't guarantee that you'll get everything the rumor mill is predicting.

5. Your Trade-In Is Worth More Today

Trade-in and resale values on older iPhones typically slide once a new generation is announced, which means the iPhone in your pocket is likely at its peak value right now. Waiting until September to upgrade would mean paying more for the new, pricier model and getting less for the old one, which would be a double hit on your wallet.

Related Roundups: iPhone 17, iPhone 18
Buyer's Guide: iPhone 17 (Neutral)

content:

How we find the best Apple Watch deals

				
					

				
			
Screenshot

Foundry




To find the best Apple Watch deals we continuously monitor the market to spot the most worthwhile bargains, but, ultimately, our recommendations are based on our own in-experience with the full range of Apple Watches, which we have reviewed and use daily. Our editors’ selections are guided by a simple principle: we won’t recommend buying something that we wouldn’t buy ourselves. We focus on products at prices we believe are fair, ensuring our recommendations steer you toward genuine savings. 
Price is just one factor we consider. A significant part of our methodology is evaluating the overall value of a deal, especially when comparing different generations of the Apple Watch. When Apple launches new models, such as the Series 11, SE 3, and Ultra 3, we see resellers clear out stock of previous-generation models at lower prices. We highlight these opportunities, as older models like the Series 10 or Ultra 2 can become excellent alternatives to the latest releases. 
It isn’t always the case that an older Watch is a good option though: we’ve previously advised readers to avoid what seemed like an all-time-low price on an older Apple Watch SE 2 because the newer Apple Watch SE 3, available for just $40 more, offered a vastly superior feature set, including an always-on display, a faster chip, and better battery life.
We also recommend checking the Apple Refurbished Store as a source for year-round discounts. Products from this store are revamped by Apple and include a one-year warranty, often providing substantial savings on recent models like the Apple Watch Series 10 or Ultra 2. 






Is now a good time to buy a new Apple Watch?

				
					

				
			
David Price / Foundry




The latest Apple Watch models – the Series 11, SE 3, and Ultra 3 – launched in September 2025, so the Series 12 and Ultra 4 are widely expected in September 2026. Unless you specifically want the Apple Watch SE 3, which isn’t expected to be updated this year, we recommend waiting.
Another reason to buy now is that Apple could increase prices when the new models launch, as it has done with other products in 2026.
If you can wait beyond the September launch, the best time to buy is during major sales events such as Amazon Prime Day (October) and Black Friday, when both new and previous-generation Apple Watches are often discounted. The worst time to buy is late summer, just before Apple’s annual September event, when you’re most likely to pay full price for a model that’s about to be replaced.
For more advice, read our guide to the best time to buy an Apple Watch. You can also find the latest discounts in our roundup of the best Apple deals right now on all Apple products, including MacBook Air deals, MacBook Pro, iMac, Mac mini, iPhone and iPad.






Which Apple Watch to buy

				
					

				
			
David Price / Foundry




When choosing which Apple Watch to buy you should consider various factors. If you need advanced health sensors, like ECG and blood oxygen monitoring, then you need to choose the Series 11 and Ultra 3 (or the Series 10 and Ultra 2) because the SE doesn’t offer those options. But if you are on a budget the Apple Watch SE 3 offers importtant features like Fall Detection and heart rate monitoring, and comes at an affordable price. The best battery life is offered by the Apple Watch Ultra 3, but the SEries 11 offers a solid 24 hours, and even the 18 hour SE 3 will be sufficient for most people, plus charging can be done in less than an hour. 
Apple Watch Series 11: This model features a 24-hour battery life, 5G connectivity on cellular models, and a more scratch-resistant display. While it shares the same S10 chip and many health features with the Series 10, its improved battery is a key advantage. Deals have been spotted offering up to $100 off, bringing the price down to as low as $279/£287
Apple Watch SE 3: This Apple Watch is the best value option for most people. The SE 3 received a major upgrade, including an always-on display, a faster chip, temperature sensing, fast charging, and 5G on cellular models. It offers most of the essential features of the Series 11 at a much lower price, starting at $249. It has been on sale for as low as $199/£197.
Apple Watch Ultra 3: Designed for adventurers and sports enthusiasts, the Ultra 3 boasts a 42-hour battery life, a larger and brighter screen, satellite connectivity for emergencies, and a rugged design. It’s the most expensive model, price drops of up to $150, to $649/£649.
Apple Watch Series 10: You may be able to find clearance deals on last-generation models. The Series 10 is very similar to the 11, with the main difference being its 18-hour battery life. It has been seen for as low as $279, a $150 discount.
Apple Watch Ultra 2: This is also a great option, with deals dropping its price by as much as $200. 
Apple Watch SE 2: If you find one on sale, we advise to avoid the Apple Watch SE 2 unless the price is exceptionally low (around $100), as the SE 3 is a far superior and more future-proof device.
Buying an older Apple Watch can be a great way to save money, but in 2026 it’s important to choose carefully. The biggest consideration is software support. Apple will drop support for several older models when watchOS 27 launches, meaning they no longer receive the latest features and may eventually miss out on security updates.
If you want an older Apple Watch that will continue to receive the latest version of watchOS, look for a model with at least an S9 chip.
Avoid buying these models and anything older, unless you’re getting them at a very low price and don’t mind running older software:
Apple Watch Series 6 (2020)
Apple Watch Series 7 (2021)
Apple Watch Series 8 (2022)
Apple Watch SE (2nd generation, 2022)
Apple Watch Ultra (1st generation, 2022)
If you’re still not sure which Apple Watch is right for your needs, take a look at our Best Apple Watch buying guide. 






Best refurbished Apple Watch deals
One of the best places to look for Apple Watch deals all year round is the Apple Refurbished Store. There, you’ll find huge Apple Watch discounts, sometimes even on the latest models. These Apple Watches have been returned to Apple for various reasons, and then completely revamped for resale. One of the major benefits of buying a refurbished Watch from Apple is that it will come with a one-year warranty so Apple may fix or replace it for free should anything happen to it in that first year.
Apple’s U.S. Refurbished Store has Apple Watch Series 11, 10 and 9, SE 3, SE 2, Ultra 2 and Ultra 3.

title: An AirPod-powered Frisbee tracker makes backyard throws scientific
content:
A developer and Frisbee fan turned AirPods into a flying-disc stat tracker, using motion sensors to measure throws in a clever demonstration of what Apple's earbuds can do beyond playing audio.

AirPods 4
Sharhan demonstrated Kinapod Disc on Reddit on July 20. The setup requires users to make a small hole in the center of a flying disc and insert one AirPod stem-first.
Once it's mounted, the AirPod sends motion data to an iPhone while Kinapod Disc detects each throw and records spin rate, total revolutions, estimated release speed, and airborne time.
It's a fun albeit expensive solution to the problem of throwing a plastic disc without enough statistics. It's also a clever use of motion sensors Apple builds into AirPods.

title: Apple Maps will be at the core of Ford's electric vehicles in 2027
content:
Apple and Ford have announced a new partnership that will see cars built on the latter's Universal Electric Vehicle platform use Apple Maps for advanced routing and future self-driving features.


Apple Maps will be key to Ford's future EV plans. Credit: Apple
The partnership will see Maps become a key component of the Universal Electric Vehicle (UEV) platform when it launches in 2027. Apple announced that its technology will go beyond simple routing and will instead be used to power Ford's next-generation BlueCruise hands-free driving feature.
"Apple Maps delivers the best map experience in the world, and we're excited to bring the power of Maps' navigation technology to Ford's innovative Universal Electric Vehicle Platform," said Eddy Cue, Apple's senior vice president of Services and Health. "With our new MapKit for Automotive SDK, we're bringing Maps further into drivers' daily lives, giving them an incredibly accurate and easy-to-use navigation system that is seamlessly integrated into Ford vehicles."

title: Apple Maps to Help Power Autonomous Driving in Ford's 2027 EV
content:
Apple and Ford today announced announced that Apple Maps will power the navigation system in Ford's upcoming Universal Electric Vehicle (UEV) Platform, launching in 2027 at a price point around $30,000.



Eddy Cue, Apple's senior vice president of Services and Health, said:

Apple Maps delivers the best map experience in the world, and we're excited to bring the power of Maps' navigation technology to Ford's innovative Universal Electric Vehicle Platform. With our new MapKit for Automotive SDK, we're bringing Maps further into drivers' daily lives, giving them an incredibly accurate and easy-to-use navigation system that is seamlessly integrated into Ford vehicles.


CarPlay and ‌CarPlay‌ Ultra. The SDK gives automakers access to Apple's mapping data and allows them to integrate navigation as a native vehicle feature rather than simply mirroring an iPhone app. Apple maintains privacy protections in automotive use, with the company not collecting users' location data or activity tied to individual identities through the vehicle's mapping system. Jim Farley, Ford Motor Company's CEO, said:

Our new midsize electric vehicle will be priced around $30,000 and redefines what advanced technology can be — simple, useful, and truly attainable for more customers. We're proud to embed Apple Maps' navigation and mapping technology directly into our Universal Electric Vehicle Platform, giving customers the ultimate navigation experience alongside our Ford app, a full suite of software, and next-generation BlueCruise, all enabled by a new zonal architecture. Apple Maps has delivered a world-class product, and we're honored to be among the first to embed it directly into a vehicle, helping define intuitive, capable driving.


Ford is among the first automakers to directly embed Apple's navigation engine rather than relying on ‌CarPlay‌. ‌CarPlay‌ will still be available on Ford vehicles, Apple said, providing an additional input method for drivers who prefer iPhone integration.

title: Mophie Roam 3-in-1 Travel Charger review: 25W Qi2.2 travel charger
content:
Macworld



At a glance
Expert's Rating



Pros
iPhone/AirPods/ Watch charge simultaneously.
Compact when folded
25W iPhone charging
StealthCharge wireless charging tech
Smart travel case
International plug adapters
Cons
Smaller rivals
Our Verdict 
The Mophie Roam stands out not just because it supports 25W Qi2.2 wireless charging, but because its StealthCharge technology keeps heat away from the charging pad, helping maintain maximum charging speeds without throttling. Rather than chasing the smallest possible design, Mophie targets premium travellers with a beautifully finished package that includes a travel case, global plug adapters and matching accessories
Price When Reviewed
This value will show the geolocated pricing text for product undefined
Best Pricing Today


Price When Reviewed
$149.95
Retailer
				
Price
				
$149.95						
View Deal						
Product
				
Price
				

								
							
						
What is it? 25W foldable 3-in-1 travel charger for Apple iPhone, Watch and AirPods.
Who is it for? iPhone 16 or later owners who want the fastest wireless charging for all their Apple mobile devices.
Who shouldn’t buy it? People not planning to travel can save on buying this package with travel pouch and plug adapters, and should instead consider the more fixed Mophie 4-in-1 wireless desktop charger that employs the same StealthCharge technology.
Many Apple users rely on their iPhone, Apple Watch and AirPods every day — all of which need charging every day, too.
We’ve reviewed the best wireless iPhone chargers and best Apple Watch chargers, which include 3-in-1 Apple chargers that handle all of them with elan.
The number of 3-in-1 travel chargers for iPhone, Watch and AirPods is growing at the same rate that their size is shrinking. Some of our favorites include the Twelve South Butterfly SE, Anker MagGo Folding, Kuxiu X40, and Satechi OnTheGo.
Rather than competing in the race to be the smallest travel charger, Mophie has focused on creating a premium travel package. The Roam combines 25W Qi2.2 wireless charging with its clever StealthCharge technology, which keeps heat away from the charging pad to help sustain maximum charging speeds without throttling. It also includes a matching zipped travel case, global plug adapters, a 40W charger and coordinated accessories for frequent travellers.

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




How compact is the Mophie Roam?
To be a bona fide “travel charger” the product must fold up to fit in a pocket and unfold to present three accessible wireless charging pads.
Like all the above travel chargers, the Mophie Roam does exactly that.

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




It’s a bi-fold, meaning it consists of two hinged sections. Two becomes three because the iPhone Qi2.2 charging pad raises into a stand (allowing for Apple’s StandBy mode), exposing the AirPods Qi pad. The Watch charging module is on the other side of the fold, with it also rising into a stand so the Watch can be seen in Nightstand mode.
Folded it measures 3 x 2.8 x 1.3 inches (76 x 71 x 33mm); unfolded it’s 6.4 inches (162mm) wide. It weighs 233g.

				
					

				
			From top left to right: Twelve South ButterFly SE, Kuxiu X40, Belkin UltraCharge Pro. From bottom left to right: Mophie 15W Travel Charger, Mophie 25W Roam Travel Charger, and Satechi OnTheGo Travel Charger.
Simon Jary




How does it compare with rival travel chargers?
There are smaller and lighter travel chargers available, but choosing between them will most likely come down to aesthetic preferences rather than cold dimensional comparisons. Even so, here are the facts:
Mophie Roam (3-in-1)
Charge: 25W/5W/5W
Size: 76 x 71 x 33mm
Weight: 233g
Colors: Black, Sail Cream
Extras: Case, 40W charger, cable, plug adapters
Price: $149.95 / £119.95
Kuxiu X40 (3-in-1)
Charge: 25W/5W/5W
Size: 64 x 64 x 18mm
Weight: 148g
Colors: Black, Gray, Wood Grain, Cosmic Orange, Dark Cherry
Extras: Case, 45W charger, cable
Price: $79.99 / £61
Anker MagGo (3-in-1)
Charge: 15W/5W/5W
Size: 60 x 60 x 36mm
Weight: 108g
Colors: Black, White, Pink, Teal
Extras: 40W charger, cable, plug adapters
Price: $89.99 / £89.99
Satechi OnTheGo (3-in-1)
Charge: 15W/5W/5W
Size: 83 x 67 x 34mm
Weight: 146g
Colors: Black, Sand, Desert Rose
Extras: Pouch, cable
Price: $99.99 / £99.99
Belkin UltraCharge Pro (2-in-1)
Charge: 25W/5W
Size: 67 x 67 x 38mm
Weight: 202g
Colors: Black, Sand
Extras: 45W charger, cable
Price: $129.95 / £129.95
Twelve South Butterfly SE (2-in-1)
Charge: 15W/5W
Size: 60 x 60 x 23mm
Weight: 105g
Colors: Black, White, Blue, Plum, Pink, Iridescent
Extras: Cable
Price: $99.99 / £79.99
Check out the individual reviews for more detail and pictures. The smallest and cheapest 3-in-1 is the Kuxiu X40, which can be raised to the tallest height. The Butterfly SE is dinkier but can simultaneously charge only two devices. The lightest is the Anker MagGo Foldable.
With its StealthCharge technology, the Roam is fastest—although not by a huge margin on the Kuxiu X40 or Belkin UltraCharge Pro.
The Roam comes with the nicest extras but is also markedly the priciest.

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




How fast does it charge?
iPhone: 25W (Qi2.2)
Apple Watch: 5W
AirPods: 5W
These are the wireless fast-charging speeds for each device. Some Watch chargers reach 7.5W, but Apple classes 5W as fast-charging for the Watch. In our tests our Watch (Series 8) charged to 50% in 25 minutes.
Only the Kuxiu X40 and its cheaper but slightly larger X55 sibling match the Mophie Roam at 25W for iPhone wireless charging, with the others on the older 15W Qi2 standard.
Note that only iPhones 16 and later support wireless 25W charging; iPhones 12-15 can still be charged using Qi2.2 chargers such as the Roam but at 15W.
Where Mophie differs is its clever StealthCharge technology that moves the main components that generate heat away from the charging pad itself to keep it as cool as possible during charging as 25W can get hot enough to force the iPhone into throttling power to protect its battery from the heat.
If kept cool, a phone should charge uninterrupted at full speed. Some chargers include fans to keep the pad and phone cool, but StealthCharge removes the need for them and so goes about its business silently. Vents at the back no doubt help too.

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




What’s included in the box?
The Mophie Roam ships with five interchangeable adapters—for North America, Europe, U.K., Australia and New Zealand, and Korea—in its rather lovely travel pouch with organizer pockets for everything including its 5ft (1.5m) USB-C cable, 40W charger and international plugs.
The pouch measures 10.9 x 3 inches (277 x 76mm).
There’s even a little weighted cable accessory to keep it all neat and tidy, which in black is the only thing in the Sail Cream-colored model that’s not matching.
Sail Cream is a lux off-white. Roam is also available in Black.

				
					

				
			
Mophie




Is the Mophie Roam worth $149?
The Mophie Roam is priced at $149.95 / £119.95, which is a decent chunk more than its rivals.
It ships with the nicest travel case and color-matched extras such as the 40W charger with international plug adapters.
Should you buy the Mophie Roam Travel Charger?
The whole package—pocketable 3-in-1 folding travel charger, travel case, charger, plug adapters, and cable—is beautifully put together, and more importantly the tech is not only the fastest possible but includes clever features that ensure that fast wireless charging speed is maintained from start to finish.

title: Google Fined $1 Billion by EU for Search Bias and App Store Restrictions
content:
Google was today fined €890 million ($1 billion) by the European Union for breaching antitrust rules with regard to its search and Google Play store services.


The European Commission found that Google had broken the Digital Markets Act (DMA) by using its dominant position in the search market to favour its own services, like shopping and hotel deals, over those of rivals.

The Commission also said that the company had abused its position by preventing developers from directing users to cheaper ways to buy apps and app subscriptions on websites and alternative app stores.

press release: 
 As part of today's two decisions, the Commission has ordered Google to bring the non-compliance to an end.

In particular, Google must implement measures to:
Treat third-party services that feature on Google's search results in a fair and non-discriminatory manner by reference to its own services, and

Allow app developers distributing their apps via Google Play Store, both technically and contractually, to freely communicate, promote offers and conclude contracts with users not only within but also outside the Google Play app store.



The Commission said that changes already made by Google to rectify the situation represented "substantial progress towards compliance." Google has also reportedly proposed and started testing changes to how it presents shopping ads and content related services, such as sports.

Google is required to comply with the Commission's decisions within 60 days, otherwise it risks periodic penalty payments of up to 5 percent of its total worldwide turnover. Google made revenues of just over $400 billion last year. It could still decide to appeal the decisions.

fined €500 million ($573 million) last year for restricting App Store developers from directing users to alternative offers, and earlier this month the EU's General Court separately rejected its challenges to its designation as a gatekeeper for the App Store and iOS.
Tags: App Store, European Commission, European Union, Google

title: Foldable iPhone Still Faces Production Hurdles, Report Says
content:
Apple's foldable iPhone still faces production hurdles that leave its exact launch timing undecided, according to a new report from DigiTimes, even as Foxconn carries out what supply chain sources describe as "final mass-production adjustments."


The report says Apple has assigned Foxconn to lead the device from new product introduction (NPI) through to mass production, citing the assembler's experience with precision structural components and large-scale production tuning. One industry source claimed Foxconn is the only Apple partner with a realistic chance of meeting the expected production target. 

began trial production in April, Apple subsequently raised its order to around 10 million units on the back of resolved hinge and circuit board problems, and just two weeks ago a China Securities Journal report aggregated by DigiTimes said the device was already in mass production with no delay envisaged. Today's report instead says the engineering validation phase may have caused a delay of one to two months, with the hinge structure and display durability testing remaining the principal bottlenecks.

iPhone 18 Pro models in September, with a book-style design, an inner display of around 7.7 to 7.8 inches, a 5.5-inch cover screen, a thickness of roughly 4.5mm, and a starting price above $2,000.

title: WhatsApp for iPad no longer requires an iPhone for setup
content:
Macworld


For an astonishing 15 years, there was no official iPad app for WhatsApp: tablet owners had to make do with one of several workarounds, such as running WhatsApp for the web in a browser. But last year Meta finally got round to releasing one.
While this was a welcome development, one limitation was that users still had to have access to a smartphone during setup. After installation on the iPad, a prompt appeared with a QR code; this had to be scanned by a phone which was already linked to the WhatsApp account. But that is no longer the case.
Meta has announced a raft of new features for WhatsApp, and these include the ability to sign up to WhatsApp directly on an iPad, without a smartphone becoming involved in any way. You’ll still need a phone number to create an account, but the registration process takes place fully on the tablet.
“Since we announced WhatsApp for iPad last year,” the post explains, “one of the biggest things we’ve heard from people is that they want to be able to create a WhatsApp account directly on their iPad, without needing to link to their phone. Now you can. Register for WhatsApp directly on the iPad app, set up your account, and you’re in.”
Aside from iPad signups, WhatsApp for CarPlay is getting a redesign, and you can share songs on Apple Music to your WhatsApp status. There’s also a new feature that lets you open and edit PDFs in the web and desktop versions of WhatsApp.
At the end of the post, Meta says the features are rolling out now, so if you’re not seeing them yet, they should be along soon.
creator: Macworld

title: EU slaps Google with a $1 billion antitrust fine

The European Union is fining Google $1 billion - image credit: EU
Just as US lawmakers ask Trump to investigate the EU's alleged "anti-American" Digital Markets Act (DMA), the European Union has used that act to fine Google. The fine is 890 million euros ($1 billion), and it's the first time Google has been fined under the DMA.
The European Commission (EC) ruled that Google favors its own services over the equivalent from rivals, "thereby breaching its obligations under the DMA."

title: macOS 27 Beta Hides Trippy Siri 'Voice Pad' Interface With 13 Voices
content:
For macOS 27 Golden Gate, Apple appears to be working on an immersive new interface for choosing Siri AI's voice, based on findings in the latest beta.


MacRumors forum member "mactracker" has shared what they call a hidden "voice pad" in the macOS 27 beta 4, which was released on Monday. When enabled, the interface replaces the System Settings menu for Siri's voice options with a nearly black canvas, with the currently selected voice marked by a colored dot. 

Pressing, holding, and moving around the pad with your pointer brings the interface to life, causing various colors to transition across the view as more dots appear and the voice modulates through different characteristics. Each dot emerges from the ether like a revealed star in a hidden constellation.

The visuals are even responsive to cursor movement via spatial audio, so that when the pointer approaches a new voice on the pad, it sounds clearer and more present, while voices further away become distant and ambient. Meanwhile, a spatial mixer tracks the pointer's distance, angle, and velocity, which adjusts volume, reverb, occlusion, and filtering across the voice and background audio layers as you move.

The pad lays out 13 voice slots in a loose grid, suggesting Apple may be preparing to support up to 13 custom voices for Siri AI. You can see the UI in action in the video embedded below.

Currently, the latest macOS 27 beta doesn't include the required voice models, so it falls back to the two custom voices available. However, a related file found in the beta describes the 13 expected American English voices by gender, age, and character, spanning everything from a soft-spoken young female voice to a deep adult male voice and two older-sounding voices.

If the interface makes the final macOS 27 cut, it would be a big jump from where things stand today. As of the current betas, American is the only voice option for Siri AI, whose expressiveness and pace can only be adjusted via sliders.

In this context, the interface likely serves as an onboarding experience for picking Siri's underlying voice, with the existing picker and its Pace and Expressivity controls used to further adjust the voice after selection. While the interface remains unfinished, it is already said to be localized for 44 languages. It also shares the same Siri setup framework with iOS 27, so we could see it on iPhone and iPad, too.



  Your browser does not support the video tag.

covered earlier this week, there's no easy way for beta testers to switch on this feature flag, as it requires disabling System Integrity Protection (SIP) and overriding internal code with a debugger.

Tag: Siri

title: Apple hasn’t given up on the iMac Ultra and neither have I
content:
Macworld


A couple of months ago, I wrote about how I’ve been pining for an iMac Ultra as a rebirth of the iMac Pro. But since then, I haven’t given it much thought. The most recent report came at the end of last year, and it was about a Mac never intended for public release. But now, Bloomberg’s Mark Gurman had this juicy single line in a new report about the new Macs:
For further in the future, Apple is working on a new iMac with an OLED screen, matching the technology coming to the high-end MacBook and, later, MacBook Air models.
Mark Gurman, Bloomberg (July 22, 2026)
That one line has led me down a path of possibilities that make me want to believe that Apple has an iMac Ultra kicking around Apple Park’s labs. And there may be no better time for such a Mac.
Making it add up
The current iMac has an LED display that’s quite nice. OLED displays are better, though. An iMac with an OLED would be a great upgrade, but it’s been reported that Apple needs to put extra effort into developing a 24-inch OLED display that meets its standards of 218 pixels per inch density and 600 nits of brightness. That leads me to believe that such a display is a costly one.
It stands to reason that a consumer-level iMac with an OLED would be more expensive than the current LED-based iMac. So maybe Apple will keep the current LED model with the base M-series chip and introduce an iMac Ultra not just with an OLED display, but also with, say, the M7 Pro chip that’s expected to debut next year, extra RAM, and a larger SSD. 
Heck, it could even have a touchscreen, creating a tie to the high-end touchscreen MacBook Pro that is supposed to ship later this year. It would be pricey, but Apple’s Ultra products are supposed to be pricey. 
Who would buy the iMac Ultra? There’s the content creation market, obviously. We use an M4 iMac to produce the Macworld Podcast, and the all-in-one design works well for the studio space. But I’d like a little more processing power, since we recently decided to produce the podcast in video instead of audio-only. A more powerful iMac Ultra with an OLED display would be perfect.
More importantly, the iMac Ultra could be another workstation that catches the eye of AI developers. While the Mac mini is the current Mac of choice, an iMac Ultra might even be a better space-saving Mac with its all-in-one design.
Apple used to make an iMac Pro, but it was discontinued five years ago. With technology that’s in development and soon to be available, it seems like the proper time for a high-end iMac comeback. Based on reports, Apple seems to agree.
creator: Macworld

title: Never mind season four; ‘Ted Lasso’ creator wants to do five and six too
content:
Macworld


The long-awaited fourth season of Ted Lasso debuts on Apple TV next month, but the creator and star of the show already has his heart set on a fifth and sixth.
In a long feature by The Hollywood Reporter, Jason Sudeikis, who plays the role of the pathologically positive soccer coach, reflects on the process which brought him from closure after the third season (“The story was told,” he says. “Ted went home to be with his son. I wasn’t going to have Ted uproot their life to justify the money truck. It made no sense to me.”) to agreeing to make a fourth. And, perhaps surprisingly, he doesn’t want to stop there.
“If all goes according to plan, the Lasso team will be back in the writers room in late August mapping out a not-yet-ordered season five,” the Reporter reports. “Once again, Sudeikis has a three-season arc in mind, and he’s hopeful that the audience will still be there.”
That’s a three-season arc beginning with season four, of course. Whereas the first three seasons focused on men’s soccer, the new episodes will revolve around A.F.C. Richmond’s women’s team. That change means the arrival of new faces and the departure of others, which Sudeikis says required some “very difficult” phone calls.
Then again, the cast probably weren’t banking on more work anyway. The last episode of season three was released on May 31, 2023 and, officially at least, Ted Lasso was finished at that point. It always seemed likely that Apple would want to do something else with such popular, bankable IP, but it was clear that a change of direction would be needed after the resolution at the end of season three. The company is likely to be just as keen on further seasons as Sudeikis, but that will depend to some extent on the reception accorded to the new episodes.
Brett Goldstein, who plays Roy Kent in the show and is also one of its writers, is optimistic. “I think [season four] has some of that season one magic,” he says. We’ll have to wait and see.
creator: Macworld

title: Kensington SD5010T5 EQ Thunderbolt 5 Dock review: Compact Thunderbolt 5 HDMI dock
content:
Macworld


At a glance
Expert's Rating



Pros
13 ports including Thunderbolt 5
Dual HDMI 2.1
140W Power Delivery
30W USB-C
2.5Gb Ethernet
Compact footprint
Cons
Only one downstream TB5
Our Verdict 
Choose the SD5010T5 if you want a compact Thunderbolt 5 dock with dual HDMI 2.1 ports, 140W laptop charging, fast 2.5Gb Ethernet, and plenty of everyday connectivity. It’s ideal for HDMI-based monitor setups that don’t require extensive Thunderbolt expansion.
Price When Reviewed
This value will show the geolocated pricing text for product undefined
Best Pricing Today


Price When Reviewed

Retailer
				
Price
				
$297.99						
View Deal						
Kensington
													
$299.99						
View Deal						
$304.99						
View Deal						
Product
				
Price
				

								
							
						
What is it? A small-footprint Thunderbolt 5 dock with two HDMI ports.
Who is it for? MacBook users who want to connect a dock to HDMI displays at the fastest bandwidth.
Who shouldn’t buy it? Owners of displays with USB-C or Thunderbolt connections, or people who need more than three displays who should look at DisplayLink docks.
Apple’s Mac mini has inspired many docks and hubs into mimicking its dimensions for an easy fit that keeps dock and Mac in as small a footprint as possible. We round these up in our How to Upgrade the Mac mini feature, but here’s a compact Thunderbolt 5 dock that isn’t an exact match but offers more ports in a neat package.
The SD5010T5 is essentially the HDMI-focused version of Kensington’s SD5000T5. Choose it if your monitors connect via HDMI and you want native dual-display support without adapters. If you prefer connecting Thunderbolt displays, high-speed storage, or other TB5 accessories, the SD5000T5’s three downstream Thunderbolt 5 ports offer greater flexibility.
How big is the SD5010T5?
The Kensington SD5010T5 measure 5.5 x 5.5 x 1.6 inches (140 x 140 x 40mm). Compare that to the Mac mini’s smaller 5 x 5 x 2 inch (127 x 127 x 52mm) footprint, and you get an idea of the Kensington dock’s compact form.
Specs and features
One upstream Thunderbolt 5 port (80Gbps, 140W)
One downstream Thunderbolt 5 port (80Gbps, 15W)
2x HDMI (2.1)
1x USB-C (10Gbps, 30W)
1x USB-C (10Gbps, 7.5W)
1x USB-A (10Gbps, 4.5W)
2x USB-A (5Gbps, 4.5W)
Ethernet (2.5Gb)
UHS-II SD card reader (312MBps)
UHS-II microSD card reader (312MBps)
3.5mm combo audio jack (front)
180W power supply

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




Kensington has sacrificed a couple of possible Thunderbolt 5 ports for two display-dedicated HDMI 2.1 ports, which is fine if your monitors host an HDMI connection. While this highly likely, three downstream Thunderbolt would have offered more flexibility although also the prospect of having to buy extra adapter cables if the display lacked a USB-C or Thunderbolt connection.
If you desire more Thunderbolt 5 ports, Kensington’s SD5000T5 Dock omits the HDMI ports for two TB5. Read our Kensington’s SD5000T5 Thunderbolt 5 Dock review to compare.
The SD5010T5 dock does have two spare USB-C ports, both at 10Gbps and one with a healthy 30W of power output; the other has 7.5W, which is at least more than the three 4.5W USB-A ports. 30W is enough to slowly charge a second MacBook or fast-charge an iPhone or iPad.

				
					

				
			
Kensington




The host MacBook can be charged at PD 3.1’s full 140W, which is powerful enough to fast-charge even a top-end 16-inch MacBook Pro.
The external power supply has a maximum 180W, which is enough for that 140W plus the 30W USB-C but not much else. As a MacBook is unlikely to be chugging back 140W, so 180W should be enough to keep this dock going without too much power throttling.
Some docks, such as Ugreen Maxidok 17-in-1 and CalDIgit TS5, come with a 240W power supply to allow for a heavier load from connected devices, but 180W is still the average.

				
					

				
			
Kensington




What display setups can it handle?
Kensington calls the SD5010T5 a “Triple 4K Docking Station”. However, the number of connected working displays depends as much on your Mac as the dock.
At the very top end, when paired with the latest M5 Pro or M5 Max chips, the SD5010T5 supports up to three external displays in Extended mode: one 6K/60Hz plus two 4K/60Hz. They can also run two 4K/60Hz plus one 4K at 144Hz (M5 Pro) or 240Hz (M5 Max).
MacBooks with M4/M5 base chips and M-Series Pro and Max chipsets, the dock supports two 4K displays at 60Hz via its two HDMI ports, or one 4K display at 60Hz and one display up to 6K via the downstream Thunderbolt 5 port. Sticking to Thunderbolt rather than HDMI might have allowed for dual 6K for Mac.
Kensington’s monitor matrix for Macs doesn’t include an 8K option for single displays. Windows laptops enjoy superior display resolutions: up to three 4K displays at 144Hz, or two 8K displays at 60Hz for Windows laptops via two HDMI ports and one Thunderbolt 5 port.

				
					

				
			Kensington’s choice of HDMI 2.1  ports for display connections means there is just the one downstream Thunderbolt 5 port.
Simon Jary




What networking features does it offer?
The Kensington SD5010T5 boasts the fast 2.5Gb Ethernet standard that is becoming the norm on top-end docking stations. It’s backwards compatible with Gigabit Ethernet (1GbE) more frequently found in homes and offices.

				
					

				
			Card readers are easily accessed from the front of the dock, as three USB ports including one 30W fast-charger.
Simon Jary




What storage options does it offer?
The SD5010T5 doesn’t have an integrated SSD slot like a growing number of docking stations, such as Satechi CubeDock, but it does host both SD and MicroSD card readers for easily accessible portable storage. Both are speedy 312MBps UHS-II and allow you to add storage as and when you need it.
You can find a 1TB MicroSD card for around $200-$270 on Amazon (£150 to £200), depending on speed.

				
					

				
			
Kensington




Can you mount this dock?
The compact dock fits Kensington’s adjustable K34050 Mounting Bracket Angle Clamp, pictured above, which allows for the dock to be raised off the desk onto cubicle walls, monitor arms, or desk edges to reduce cable clutter and maximize workspace.

				
					

				
			
Simon Jary




Is the Kensington SD5010T5 worth its $299 price?
The Kensington SD5010T5 EQ Thunderbolt 5 Dock is priced at $299.99. The £335 Amazon U.K. price seems high in comparison.
This is a decent price for a Thunderbolt 5 dock without an SSD slot, which would add around another $100 to the price tag. If you fancy faster extra storage than you’ll get from an SD card, then consider one of these SSD-packing docks in our roundup of the best Thunderbolt docks. Kensington has its own 19-port SSD-slotted EQ Pro Thunderbolt 5 Dock (SD7100TS) for $449.
Another Kensington alternative is the similar Kensington SD5000T5 Thunderbolt 5 Dock, which has two fewer ports for the same price. While that seems a rough deal, it’s a different setup, with three downstream Thunderbolt 5 ports, one with a 60W power output, rather than the SD5010T5’s dual HDMI ports.
Should you buy the Kensington SD5010T5 EQ Thunderbolt 5 Dock?
Kensington has two similar Thunderbolt 5 docks: this square SD5010T5 with two dedicated HDMI ports and one downstream Thunderbolt 5 port, and the long SD5000T5 with three downstream Thunderbolt 5 ports but no HDMI. If your chosen displays demand HDMI, this dock is a good choice and we like the compact footprint, too. But if you want the flexibility of Thunderbolt displays and devices, the sibling SD5000T5 looks a wiser choice.

title: You Can Now Recover Your Google Account With a Selfie Video
content:
Google today introduced a new selfie sign-in option for Google Account recovery. It offers an alternative way for Google users to access their accounts alongside passkeys, recovery contacts, and other entry methods.


To create a selfie video, you look at your device's camera and then follow Google's instructions to complete a series of head movements to capture multiple angles. The video is saved to your account and can be used later if you need access.

Google recommends users have multiple sign-in methods set up to avoid losing access to an account. With selfie video, you can take a selfie to get access to your account if you don't have access to your email address or a trusted device. Google compares your new selfie video to the one saved to the account to grant access. Multiple layers of security are meant to prevent impersonation attempts like AI-generated photos and videos.

Google says selfie videos are encrypted and stored securely, and are only used for helping you sign in to your account, unless you opt to "share it for additional purposes." Videos can be deleted anytime.

Tag: Google

title: Time’s up, Android, Apple Watch won
content:
Macworld


It’s been almost 12 years since Apple unveiled its smartwatch to the world, and if you compare that first model to the newest Series 11, you’ll see that a lot has changed. Apple Watch has become slimmer, larger, and packed with more sensors. Even so, Apple continues to update its Watch every year with incremental improvements.
Now, when a new Apple Watch arrives, the reaction is more like, “Is anything new?” In fact, the latest models don’t differ much from one another, and some people are wondering when the next big change to the Apple Watch will come.
The thing is, it might be a long time before we see another radical change coming to the Apple Watch. That doesn’t mean Apple has stopped innovating, but rather that the Apple Watch has matured enough to establish itself as the leading smartwatch maker and one of the top wristwatch makers in the world. And while its Android competitors might think they’re keeping pace, they’re actually very far behind.
A mature platform
When the original Apple Watch launched in 2015, the entire category felt experimental. Apple’s early vision for the product wasn’t even entirely clear. The company talked about communication, fashion, fitness, productivity, apps, and luxury all at the same time during its introductory keynote.
Over time, the usage patterns of Apple Watch buyers revealed what people really wanted. Most users didn’t care about the luxury factor, nor did they want to replace their smartphones entirely. They simply wanted something to do basic things like check notifications and other quick updates without pulling out their phone.

				
					

				
			
The Apple Watch has a familiar, easy-to-use interface that’s been crafted over a decade of evolution.

Foundry




Apple also discovered that users were most interested in health and fitness features. By the time the Apple Watch Series 2 was released, Apple already had a clearer vision of what the future of the Apple Watch should look like.
The Apple Watch can now take ECGs (Series 4), track blood oxygen levels (Series 6), measure wrist temperature (Series 8), and much more. The interface has also been continuously updated to make it more practical and convenient, showing useful information at a glance. At the same time, the design now looks more modern, yet still quite familiar.
But the excitement between generations is largely gone. For instance, the Apple Watch Series 11 introduced 5G connectivity, slightly better battery life, and improved durability. These are nice-to-have improvements, but they certainly don’t sound exciting to many people.
When people complain that Apple Watch updates have become boring, they’re not entirely wrong. The differences between recent generations are smaller than they used to be. But that’s also true for many mature product categories.
Smartphones no longer evolve as dramatically as they did 10 years ago. The same applies to laptops. Once companies figure out the ideal balance between hardware and software, improvements become more focused on refinement rather than reinvention. The Apple Watch seems to have reached that point.

				
					

				
			
The Apple Watch design has evolved, but there are few notable changes between generations.

Foundry




For instance, the transition from the original Apple Watch to the Series 4 felt huge. Apple introduced a significantly larger display, a more modern design, and an ECG sensor. The evolution from Series 4 to Series 11, however, feels much smaller. And honestly, that’s fine.
The Apple Watch already does most things people expect from a smartwatch extremely well. Notifications are reliable, fitness tracking works great, health features have become incredibly advanced, and the integration with the iPhone remains unmatched.
Competition lagging behind
Looking at the smartwatch landscape, it’s clear that other smartwatches have also improved significantly. For Android users, there are great options such as Google’s Pixel Watch. Garmin remains unbeatable when it comes to battery life and advanced fitness metrics. And just this week, Samsung unveiled the Galaxy Watch 9 and Galaxy Watch Ultra 2 with AI fitness coaching and more accurate health tracking. 
Even so, none of these products can beat the full Apple Watch experience on its own. The Apple Watch still feels like the default smartwatch for most people, especially for iPhone users. In fact, it’s hard to think of another wearable that integrates so seamlessly and simply with any other ecosystem. 
This is due, in large part, to the advantages of Apple’s Continuity features. Users love features like unlocking their Mac with their Apple Watch, syncing health data across devices, or quickly pinging their iPhone with just a tap, not to mention the integration with Apple Fitness+. Competitors still struggle to fully replicate that experience.

				
					

				
			
None of Apple Watch’s competitors can compete with the smarts and simplcity of watchOS.

Foundry




That’s why the Apple Watch feels so mature today. It’s no longer trying to prove itself with experimental ideas or flashy concepts. It has already figured out what most people expect from a smartwatch, and now it simply does those things better than anyone else.
Plus, Apple just announced watchOS 27, which brings Apple Intelligence and the new Siri AI to the Apple Watch for the first time. Gemini support was one of the main advantages of buying a Pixel or Galaxy watch, and even though the new Siri will require a nearby iPhone, it takes away one of the few reasons to consider an Android watch.
Some companies are taking a very different approach, such as Samsung with the Galaxy Ring, Google with the screenless Fitbit Air, and Garmin’s new CIRQA Smart Band. Still, these products don’t have the same appeal as the Apple Watch. They just feel incomplete compared to a fully integrated communication device.
Room for improvement
At this point, Apple doesn’t need another major Apple Watch redesign. It doesn’t need a camera on your wrist or some other flashy feature that only looks impressive in advertisements.
What the Apple Watch really needs now is to continue gaining quality-of-life improvements. Specifically, better battery life.
The Apple Watch battery has improved over the years, with fast charging support arriving in 2021 and last year’s models getting a significant battery life boost to 24 hours. But after almost 12 years, it’s still a device that requires daily charging, forcing users to find charging windows during the day. And while most Apple Watch owners adapted to this routine, it’s still the biggest compromise of the product.

				
					

				
			
The Apple Watch is iconic in a way no other smartwatch is.

Foundry




Users are no longer asking Apple to reinvent what the smartwatch is supposed to do. Most users already seem satisfied with the overall experience. What they want now are improvements that make the device more practical.
These changes may not sound all that exciting, but they would improve the Apple Watch experience far more than a dramatic redesign or a radical hardware overhaul. This just highlights how mature the Apple Watch has become.
Apple doesn’t need to reinvent the Apple Watch because it already figured out what most people want from a smartwatch. At this point, the future of the Apple Watch isn’t about a dramatic change, but rather about refining what already works very well.
creator: Macworld

title: Apple prepares ‘virtual shopping assistant’ to help you spend money
content:
Macworld


Following the comparatively triumphant debut of Siri AI this summer, Apple is preparing to use AI to power something even more important: shopping.
As spotted by MacRumors, the privacy policy for the Apple Store app reveals the existence of a new “virtual shopping assistant.” Such a tool is not yet available in any public-facing capacity, but the text strongly suggests it will imminently be offered in certain regions as part of the Apple Store app.
The policy naturally focuses on the privacy elements of the assistant rather than its functionality. Here’s what we know so far:
“Where the Apple Store app virtual shopping assistant is available, Apple collects and stores your account information, device identifiers, carrier information, chat information, and where enabled, location data to personalize the chat experience, provide relevant responses, and if you opt in, to improve the virtual shopping assistant. Before sharing the chat with our partners, Apple scrubs personal identifiers from it. These partners will use this information only to help Apple to provide a conversational response. Apple will save these transcripts to make them available when you return to the Apple Store app, for our business analytics, and if you opt in, to improve the virtual shopping assistant.”
From that we can deduce that Apple will likely use third-party AI to power the tool: hence the references to “partners” and the necessity to share chats with them “to help Apple to provide a conversational response.” What isn’t clear is what the assistant will do to, well, assist.
Given that it has access to the customer’s account, device, and carrier information, the assistant would be capable of offering advice about which Apple products would be compatible with their existing setup, and potentially about which would be most suitable for their needs. It would also have access to more general advice given to all customers. Whether this latter advice would be designed to benefit the customer or the company is more open to interpretation.
In a Reddit thread discussing MacRumors’ article, while some commenters think the assistant may be useful for older and/or less tech-savvy customers, others are skeptical about its impartiality and ability to give unbiased advice; one suggests its purpose may be “to walk you toward AppleCare and a trade-in on the way out.” It’s worth bearing in mind, however, that human employees at bricks-and-mortar Apple Stores already have the same incentive to upsell and far more ability to do so, yet have historically tended not to be too pushy. A bigger worry may be the potential for hallucinations and bad advice, which Apple will need to anticipate with human oversight.
Regardless of such concerns, the Cupertino AI bandwagon shows no sign of slowing down. Apple is also planning to use AI to transcribe Genius Bar sessions, and for a chatbot used by retail partners.

title: Mac users can get Microsoft Office and Windows 11 Pro for life for $165
content:
Macworld


TL;DR: Get Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Windows 11 Pro, and more together for life for $165.
Last year, Microsoft ended support for Windows 10 and raised the price for Microsoft 365 to $99.99/year. For Mac users running Windows software through apps like Parallels Pro, that was especially frustrating. If you want to replace your subscription and upgrade your OS, you can now get a bundle with Microsoft Office 2024 and Windows 11 Pro together for $164.97 (reg. $448.99).
Office 2024 runs natively on your Mac and gives you Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Outlook, and OneNote for a single payment. All five apps work offline, your files sync once you reconnect, and feature updates come at no extra cost. Because this is the most recent version of Office, it also comes with the new AI integrations like Smart Compose for Word and Dynamic Arrays for Excel.
Windows 11 Pro handles the software that only runs on Windows. Install it inside Parallels Pro, and those apps open right on your Mac. BitLocker encrypts the whole drive, so a lost or stolen laptop doesn’t hand over your files. Hyper-V spins up a separate virtual machine when you want to test something without touching your main setup, and Windows Sandbox gives you a throwaway space to open a risky file, then wipe it the moment you close the window.
Replace your Microsoft 365 subscription and upgrade your OS in one go.
Get Microsoft Office 2024 and Windows 11 Pro for life for $164.97.

title: 12th-Generation iPad: Everything We Know About Apple's Next Budget Tablet
content:
Apple is working on a 12th-generation iPad, and plans to update the most affordable tablet in the ‌iPad‌ in 2027. It's not going to be a major update, but if you're planning a purchase, you might want to wait for the newest model.


Design

There are no rumors of an updated design for the 12th-generation ‌iPad‌, so it will likely have the same 11-inch edge-to-edge display with Touch ID Side Button and thick bezels.

iPad Pro and iPad Air models.

The low-cost ‌iPad‌ is only available in a single screen size, and it is Apple's thickest tablet at 7mm. It features a Retina LED display with no ProMotion support, no P3 wide color, and no lamination (display technology that allows for improved visuals) compared to Apple's other tablets.

Apple makes the low-cost ‌iPad‌ in several fun colors, and we could see some new shades in 2026. Current colors include blue, pink, silver, and yellow.

Apple Pencil.
A-Series Processor

Rumors suggest the low-cost ‌iPad‌ will use Apple's A18 or A19 chip, both of which are built on a 3-nanometer process to bring speed and efficiency improvements over the A16 chip that's in the current model.

Apple Intelligence, but the A18 and A19 do, so that will mark a major update for Apple's affordable tablet. The 2026 model should be able to support ‌Apple Intelligence‌ features that are unavailable with the 2025 model.

The updated chips also offer hardware-accelerated ray tracing and similar features for improved gameplay. The 11th-generation ‌iPad‌ has 6GB RAM, but Apple will need to bump that up to 8GB for ‌Apple Intelligence‌.
N1 Chip

Apple debuted its own Wi-Fi and Bluetooth chip, called the N1, in the iPhone 17 models. The N1 was added to the ‌iPad Pro‌, and it's possible that Apple plans to add the chip to all future devices coming in 2026 and beyond.

The N1 chip supports Wi-Fi 7, Bluetooth 6, and Thread networking technology for smart home devices.

its more premium devices, based on leaked internal code. If that's the case, the ‌iPad‌ won't include the N1 chip.
Apple Modem

Apple has designed C1 and C1X modem chips that it has used in iPhones and iPads in 2025, and the next-generation version of the ‌iPad‌ could also get an Apple-designed modem chip for cellular models.

Apple's modem chips are more power efficient than Qualcomm chips while providing similar performance.
Pricing

With Apple's June price increase, the 11th-generation ‌iPad‌ is priced starting at $449 for 128GB of storage, which is up $100 from the previous price. Apple raised prices because of increasing RAM costs, and there are no indications that component costs are coming down before the 12th-generation model is set to launch.

It's likely it will have the same $449 starting price.
Launch Date

Bloomberg initially suggested Apple would refresh the ‌iPad‌ sometime in 2026, but now it looks like it's not going to get an update until the first quarter of 2027 at the earliest.

content:
Apple's privacy policy for the Apple Store app suggests it will soon get a virtual shopping assistant to help users decide what to purchase.


The app will have a chat interface where users can ask questions about Apple products, with Apple using data like account information, device identifiers, chat information, carrier information, and location data to offer a personalized experience. Transcripts of chats are saved for later reference.

Apple says that information is sent to its "partners" to help Apple provide a conversational response, which suggests Apple might be using a third-party AI model for the virtual assistant.
Where the Apple Store app virtual shopping assistant is available, Apple collects and stores your account information, device identifiers, carrier information, chat information, and where enabled, location data to personalize the chat experience, provide relevant responses, and if you opt in, to improve the virtual shopping assistant. Before sharing the chat with our partners, Apple scrubs personal identifiers from it. These partners will use this information only to help Apple to provide a conversational response. Apple will save these transcripts to make them available when you return to the Apple Store app, for our business analytics, and if you opt in, to improve the virtual shopping assistant.

The virtual shopping assistant interface is not live in the ‌Apple Store‌ app yet, though it is possible Apple is testing the feature with some users. Apple also added a virtual assistant to its Apple Support app, but that feature still hasn't rolled out to everyone.
Tag: Apple Store
This article, "Apple Store App Getting AI Shopping Assistant" first appeared on MacRumors.com

title: Second iOS 27 and iPadOS 27 Public Betas Now Available
content:
Apple today released the second public betas of iOS 27 and iPadOS 27, allowing anyone with a compatible device to download and test the new software. The second betas come a week after Apple seeded the first betas. 


You can get started by signing up on Apple's beta website and then opting in to the public beta by going to Settings > General > Software Update and choosing the ‌iOS 27‌ or ‌iPadOS 27‌ Public Beta option. 

Siri AI, a more capable version of ‌Siri‌ that can search the web, access your personal information, see what's on your screen, and take actions in and across apps. ‌Siri‌ in ‌iOS 27‌ is more similar to Claude or ChatGPT than the prior version of ‌Siri‌, with a dedicated ‌Siri‌ app for back-and-forth conversations. You can use ‌Siri‌ AI if you have a device that supports Apple Intelligence.

Visual Intelligence moved to the Camera app and supports new features like getting the nutritional information for a meal or splitting a bill, and ‌Siri‌ is able to generate text for you or help you refine your writing with Write with ‌Siri‌.

Photos app, Safari can organize your tabs automatically, and you can create Shortcuts and Safari extensions using natural language commands. AirPods have custom EQ, the Home app supports 4K video cameras, and there are new child safety controls for parents.

Apple made dozens of improvements to device performance, and everything feels faster, even on older iPhones. Apps launch quicker, AirDrop transfers are faster, the keyboard loads quicker, and more.

dedicated iOS 27 roundup, with info on ‌iPadOS 27‌ available in our separate iPadOS 27 roundup.
Related Roundups: iOS 27, iPadOS 27
